Curriculum → Caching, asynchrony, and resilience → Idempotency, retries, and back pressure

Lesson 4 of 5

Idempotency, retries, and back pressure

Make distributed workflows safe under duplicate delivery, partial failure, and overload instead of assuming every call succeeds exactly once.

25-35 min Caching, asynchrony, and resilience 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

These controls separate systems that merely work in demos from systems that remain correct during incidents.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Design for repeated attempts Payment and order creation should accept an idempotency key2. Retry with restraint Use exponential backoff with jitter3. Back pressure protects the system Use admission control and per-tenant fairness

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing These controls separate systems that merely work in demos from systems that remain correct during incidents. 2. Design for repeated attempts Payment and order creation should accept an idempotency key3. Retry with restraint Use exponential backoff with jitter4. Back pressure protects the system Use admission control and per-tenant fairness
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

These controls separate systems that merely work in demos from systems that remain correct during incidents.

What to say first

  • State how duplicate requests are made safe.
  • Use retries with bounded budgets and jitter.
  • Explain how the system rejects or defers excess work.
2

Study section

Design for repeated attempts

Retries happen because networks are unreliable and callers time out. Idempotency keys, request deduplication, and state-machine design prevent duplicate side effects.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Payment and order creation should accept an idempotency key
  • Consumers should tolerate at-least-once delivery
  • Make retryable operations explicit in the API
3

Study section

Retry with restraint

Retries improve availability only when bounded by timeouts, jitter, and an understanding of what failures are likely to recover.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use exponential backoff with jitter
  • Avoid retry storms during brownouts
  • Fail fast on validation errors or non-retryable conflicts
4

Study section

Back pressure protects the system

When a dependency is overloaded, you should shed load, queue selectively, or degrade features instead of letting every layer time out together.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use admission control and per-tenant fairness
  • Surface retry-after semantics when possible
  • Circuit breakers and load shedding can preserve core traffic

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • State how duplicate requests are made safe.
  • Use retries with bounded budgets and jitter.
  • Explain how the system rejects or defers excess work.
  • Protect the most important traffic first during overload.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Retrying everything blindly.
  • Treating at-least-once delivery as if it were exactly-once.
  • Allowing low-value traffic to consume all capacity during incidents.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you make a payment API safe to retry?
  • When should a service shed load instead of queueing?
  • What is the difference between retry logic and true idempotency?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Idempotency, retries, and back pressure

Push retries, queues, and cache behavior until the resilience pattern becomes visible in the metrics. Apply it to idempotency, retries, and back pressure so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0.0 KB markdown

Explain the concept

Teach "Idempotency, retries, and back pressure" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would you make a payment API safe to retry?
  • Design for repeated attempts
  • Retry with restraint
  • State how duplicate requests are made safe.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
